questions.question: Ranked from least to most penetrating in matter, the correct order is:
options: Gamma, beta, alpha
Alpha, beta, then gamma/X and neutron
Beta, alpha, neutron
Neutron, gamma, alpha
questions.explanation: Alpha is stopped by paper or the dead skin layer, beta by a few millimeters of aluminum, while gamma, X, and neutron radiation penetrate deeply and are only attenuated, never fully stopped.

questions.question: A sealed Ir-192 source fails its leak test, showing removable activity above the typical threshold. What has fundamentally changed about the hazard?
options: Nothing — the gamma output is unchanged, so the hazard is identical
The device is now an external *and* internal hazard: escaping radioactive material can contaminate surfaces and be inhaled or ingested, so the source is removed from service and the RSO notified
The source becomes a neutron emitter
Only the transport paperwork changes
questions.explanation: A leaking capsule converts a purely external radiation hazard into a contamination problem — radioactive material that can spread and enter the body, where quality factors are far harsher. That is the entire purpose of periodic leak testing.

questions.question: Which set of quality factors correctly reflects relative biological effectiveness as used in dose-equivalent calculations?
options: Gamma 10, beta 10, neutron 1, alpha 1
Gamma and beta about 1, neutrons about 10, alpha about 20
All radiation types are weighted equally at 1
Gamma 20, neutron 1, alpha 10
questions.explanation: Equal absorbed doses are not equally harmful: densely ionizing radiation does more biological damage, so neutrons carry a quality factor near 10 and internally deposited alpha about 20, while gamma, X, and beta are about 1.

questions.question: A source produces 900 mR/h at 1 meter. At 3 meters the dose rate is about:
options: 100 mR/h
300 mR/h
90 mR/h
450 mR/h
questions.explanation: Inverse square: tripling the distance cuts the rate by a factor of nine — 900 ÷ 9 = 100 mR/h.

questions.question: A 100 Ci Ir-192 source (≈ 0.5 R/h per curie at 1 m) is exposed unshielded and uncollimated. Using distance alone, roughly how far away does the dose rate fall to a typical 2 mR/h boundary level?
options: About 16 meters
About 50 meters
About 160 meters
About 500 meters
questions.explanation: 50 R/h at 1 m is 50,000 mR/h; by inverse square, d = √(50,000 ÷ 2) ≈ 158 m. The impractical size of that circle is exactly why collimation and shielding are used on real sites.

questions.question: Which statement about warning systems in a typical permanent exposure room is correct?
options: Either a visible or an audible signal is provided, but never both, to avoid confusion
Visible and audible signals indicate when the source is exposed, are designed to fail safe, and are function-checked before use each day
Warning signals are optional if the operator carries a survey meter
The area radiation monitor replaces the need for any entrance interlock
questions.explanation: Typical requirements layer visible beacons and audible signals on top of the entrance interlock and an independent area monitor — all fail-safe and verified operational before exposures begin.

questions.question: Which pairing of unit and quantity is correct?
options: Gray — exposure in air
Sievert — dose equivalent
Coulomb per kilogram — absorbed dose
Rem — activity of a source
questions.explanation: The sievert (100 rem) measures dose equivalent; the gray measures absorbed dose, coulomb per kilogram measures exposure in air, and activity is measured in curies or becquerels.

questions.question: Why does work around a neutron source require different personnel dosimetry than routine gamma radiography?
options: Neutron dose is always too small to matter
Standard gamma instruments and badges barely respond to neutrons, so neutron-capable devices such as neutron pocket dosimeters, albedo TLDs, or track-etch detectors are required
Neutrons destroy TLD crystals on contact
Film badges over-respond to neutrons and would overstate the dose
questions.explanation: A gamma quartz-fiber dosimeter can read near zero while real neutron dose accumulates — neutron fields demand instruments and badges designed to register neutron interactions.

questions.question: Your pocket dosimeter reads high but on-scale at 150 mR after a shift you believe involved little exposure, and you suspect the unit leaked charge. The professional response is to:
options: Log a more reasonable estimate based on your partner's reading
Report the questionable reading to the RSO so the dose can be investigated and the instrument checked — never certify your own exposure estimate
Re-zero the dosimeter and forget it, since it stayed on-scale
Swap the dosimeter for a spare and discard the reading
questions.explanation: Questionable or unexplained readings go to the RSO for investigation; the worker does not get to decide unilaterally that a recorded dose was false, because the record protects them.

questions.question: Under typical NRC / Agreement-State rules, the dose limits protecting members of the public from licensed operations are:
options: 0.1 rem per year, and no more than 2 mrem in any one hour in unrestricted areas
5 rem per year, the same as radiation workers
0.5 rem per month with no hourly restriction
There are no public dose limits, only worker limits
questions.explanation: The public limit in the typical model is one-fiftieth of the worker limit — 0.1 rem per year — with a 2 mrem-in-any-hour cap that field boundaries and surveys are built to protect.
